clusters.title: Patient records stolen in cyber-attack on Australian healthcare provider
clusters.neutral_headline: Patient records stolen in cyber-attack on Australian healthcare provider
bullet_summary: Partnered Health, owned by private equity firm Quadrant, said 21 clinics across several cities including Sydney, Melbourne and Canberra have been affected by the breach which occurred on 23 June
Reported by Guardian Australia, The Age, ABC News Australia

items.title: Patient records stolen in cyber-attack on Australian healthcare provider
items.standfirst: Partnered Health says personal information and medical details and notes were breached in the attack on 21 clinicsGet our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcastThousands of medical records and patient information stored with one of Australia’s biggest healthcare providers have been breached in a cyber-attack.Partnered Health, owned by private equity firm Quadrant, said 21 clinics across several cities including Sydney, Melbourne and Canberra have been affected by the breach which occurred on 23 June. Continue reading...

items.title: Personal information stolen in new cyberattack on health clinics
items.standfirst: Personal information from 21 health clinics nationwide has been stolen in a new cyberattack on Australian healthcare provider Partnered Health.
items.content: Personal information from 21 health clinics nationwide has been stolen in a new cyberattack on Australian healthcare provider Partnered Health.

items.title: GP clinic network hit by major cyber breach
items.standfirst: Partnered Health, which operates more than 50 general practice and skin cancer clinics across Australia, has identified more than a dozen clinics where it fears patient information was taken.
items.content: Partnered Health, which operates more than 50 general practice and skin cancer clinics across Australia, has identified more than a dozen clinics where it fears patient information was taken.

clusters.title: Former Sydney church turned LGBTQ+ arts venue weighs legal action after landlord orders it to stop ‘offensive trade’
clusters.neutral_headline: Ex-Sydney church turned LGBT nightclub ordered to 'cease offensive trading'
bullet_summary: The LGBTQIA+ venue said it was exploring its legal options after being issued with a breach of lease notice just days after opening at a former Sydney church
Reported by Guardian Australia, ABC News Australia

items.title: Former Sydney church turned LGBTQ+ arts venue weighs legal action after landlord orders it to stop ‘offensive trade’
items.standfirst: Divine Playhouse promoter and founder says venue faces significant operational, legal and financial challengesGet our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcastA former church turned arts venue at the centre of a culture war is exploring legal action after receiving a notice from its landlord that forced its organisers to close the space and cancel planned events.Divine Playhouse is facing possible eviction, with its landlord ordering the venue’s organisers, Heaps Gay Events, to stop “engaging in offensive trade”, days after religious groups protested its opening night. Continue reading...
items.content: Divine Playhouse promoter and founder says venue faces significant operational, legal and financial challengesGet our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcastA former church turned arts venue at the centre of a culture war is exploring legal action after receiving a notice from its landlord that forced its organisers to close the space and cancel planned events.Divine Playhouse is facing possible eviction, with its landlord ordering the venue’s organisers, Heaps Gay Events, to stop “engaging in offensive trade”, days after religious groups protested its opening night. Continue reading...

items.content: The LGBTQIA+ venue said it was exploring its legal options after being issued with a breach of lease notice just days after opening at a former Sydney church.

items.title: LGBTQI+ artists have been targeted for ‘insulting Christianity’. To queer people, this feels particularly cruel | James Thorpe
items.standfirst: A theatre pop-up in a former Sydney church has been accused of ‘offensive trade’. But if causing offence can lead to eviction, every artist is vulnerableArts venue faces eviction from former Sydney church after religious groups protest LGBTQ+ eventsA priest and two nuns walk into a bar. In Sydney this week, this wasn’t the setup for a bad joke but for a fiery culture war.The bar in question is within a long-deconsecrated church, and the City of Sydney is considering an application for its redevelopment into luxury apartments. Divine Playhouse, which opened to the public last Wednesday, is a year-long queer-friendly and inclusive arts pop-up, supported by the New South Wales government and the City of Sydney. Continue reading...
items.content: A theatre pop-up in a former Sydney church has been accused of ‘offensive trade’. But if causing offence can lead to eviction, every artist is vulnerableArts venue faces eviction from former Sydney church after religious groups protest LGBTQ+ eventsA priest and two nuns walk into a bar. In Sydney this week, this wasn’t the setup for a bad joke but for a fiery culture war.The bar in question is within a long-deconsecrated church, and the City of Sydney is considering an application for its redevelopment into luxury apartments. Divine Playhouse, which opened to the public last Wednesday, is a year-long queer-friendly and inclusive arts pop-up, supported by the New South Wales government and the City of Sydney. Continue reading...

clusters.title: Government moves on AI data centres and copyright
clusters.neutral_headline: Government moves on AI data centres and copyright

items.title: Government moves on AI data centres and copyright
items.standfirst: The prime minister has unveiled Labor's plan for new AI national standards, warning Australia has a narrow window to set AI's "social licence". A new set of standards for artificial intelligence will provide clear rules for data centres, forcing them to minimise water usage and underwrite or supply their own power. Anthony Albanese has vowed AI companies will not be able to use Australian books, music, art or news to train AI "without the artist's control".
items.content: The prime minister has unveiled Labor's plan for new AI national standards, warning Australia has a narrow window to set AI's "social licence". A new set of standards for artificial intelligence will provide clear rules for data centres, forcing them to minimise water usage and underwrite or supply their own power. Anthony Albanese has vowed AI companies will not be able to use Australian books, music, art or news to train AI "without the artist's control".
